title: The power ranking of the members of the Agricultural Committee of the European Parliament
authors: Imre Fertő; László Á Kóczy; Attila Kovács; Balázs R Sziklai; Imre Ferto; Laszlo A. Koczy; Balazs Sziklai Sziklai
abstract: We aim to identify the most influential members of the Agricultural Committee of the European Parliament (COMAGRI). Unlike previous studies that were based on case studies or interviews with stakeholders, we analyse the voting power of MEPs using a spatial Banzhaf power index. We identify critical members: members whose votes are necessary to form winning coalitions. We found that rapporteurs, EP group coordinators and MEPs from countries with high relative Committee representations, such as Ireland, Poland or Romania are powerful actors. Italy emerges as the most influential member state, while France seems surprisingly weak.

title: Sweeping the flies away: evidence from a fruit fly eradication program
authors: Lina Salazar; Julian Aramburu; Marcos Agurto; Alessandro Maffioli; Jossie Fahsbender; Lina Salazar
abstract: This article evaluates the short-term impacts of a fruit fly integrated pest management program in Peru. Exploiting arbitrary variation in the program’s intervention borders, we use a geographical regression discontinuity design to identify the program’s effects on agricultural outcomes. Pre-treatment balance tests show that producer and farm-level pre-treatment characteristics evolve smoothly at the intervention border. Results indicate that farmers within treated areas improved pest knowledge and are more likely to implement prevention and control practices. Also, they increased fruit production and sales. Our findings are confirmed by placebo tests and are robust to alternative regression discontinuity bandwidths and polynomials.

title: A Collaborative Investigation into Methods of Chemical Analysis of Ground Pyrethrum Flowers
authors: George V. Dyke; Emily P. Simpson
abstract: This article is of wide interest because it illustrates how a planned investigation with a statistical analysis of the results can disclose the sources of variations in the results of chemical analyses and give measures of the errors. Readers who are interested only in the practical conclusions may confine their attention to Part I; Part II is for those who are interested also in the statistical basis of the conclusions.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Statistical Approach to the Specification of Plastics
abstract: Although this article is primarily concerned with plastics, the underlying general principles apply to other materials. The usual type of specification, in which each batch of material is accepted or rejected according as its tested quality exceeds or falls below a specified level, presents difficulties. These are overcome in the new B.S. 771 for moulding material by regarding groups of batches as wholes and taking account of variability in quality as well as average levels. Mr. Wainwright proposes a somewhat similar but simplified form of specification suitable for laminated sheets.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Statistician Looks at the International Management Congress of 1951
abstract: As Mr. Swan was going to the Brussels conference he was invited to write his impressions from the point of view of the applied statistician. Mr. Swan says that a certain amount of time was devoted to the use of statistical thinking as a tool for management, and infers from the interest expressed that more attention will have to be given to statistics at the next conference. This time statistics entered into the discussions of work measurement, quality control, and job evaluation.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Study of the Lives of Pots Used in Making Glass
abstract: It was required to learn something of the factors that influence the life of clay pots used in making glass, and only the statistically ‘unbalanced’ records of two years' actual working were available as data. Miss Keen and Mr. Page describe how they solved the problem by repeatedly applying a simple method of analysis.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Use for the Jury Qualification in Sample Design
abstract: The proportion of the electors liable for jury service is an index of economic status of an area that can be determined from the Register of Electors for any area, small or large, definable in terms of streets or roads. It is useful in designing sample schemes.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Calibration of an Integrating Gas Flowmeter
abstract: This article is an interesting fellow to Mr. Jessop's article one Line or Two? since it illustrates by a practical example the problem of choosing the most suitable line in regression analysis. It also illustrates the treatment of heterogeneous data. Mr. Spendley describes how corresponding readings of gas flow measured by two types of flowmeter were used to obtain a calibration curve for one of the types and to estimate the errors involved in using this curve.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Composite Mean Squares and Their Degrees of Freedom
authors: Leroy P. V. Johnson; Ernest S. Keeping
abstract: This article calls attention to a relatively little‐known method for dealing with a situation that sometimes arises when treating experimental results by the analysis of variance. It is intended for readers who are acquainted with the standard methods of analysis.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Estimating Individual Expenditure from Family Totals
abstract: Surveys commonly give estimates of the total expenditure of various types of families and it is sometimes desired to deduce from these estimates of the share to be apportioned to typical individuals. This is the problem dealt with in this article. Mr. Kemsley describes and discusses several methods, which have a wider application than to estimates of expenditure, and gives some results which are interesting in themselves.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Estimating the Future Population
abstract: It is important for many purposes not only to know the present size of the population and its composition, but also to make estimates of future trends. After dealing with methods of making such estimates Mr. Cox gives some of the projections of the population in Great Britain made for the Royal Commission on Population and discusses them in the light of such results as are available from the 1951 census.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Estimating the Social Class of Towns
abstract: A measure of the social class of towns that is useful to businessmen and economists is the percentage of households in which the chief wage‐earner has a basic wage exceeding £7 10s. a week. Data are not available for determining this directly, but this article shows how the measure may be estimated from available data and gives a table of values for 140 large towns.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Facts from Figures
abstract: M.J. Moroney: Facts from Figures. Harmondsworth: Pelican Books, 1951. Pp. 472, 714in.×412in. 5s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Index Numbers of Retail Prices 1938–51
abstract: This article is based largely on the work of the Cost of Living Technical Committee as summarised in the recently published Report on the Working of the Interim Index of Retail Prices. Here index numbers are calculated with alternative weights. Links are worked out for extending back to June 1947 and to 1938 the new series of index numbers with January 1952 as 100, as published by the Ministry of Labour. Professor Allen is a member of the Technical Committee.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: One Line or Two?
abstract: Many an experimenter has tried to use the method of least squares to estimate the true straight‐line relationship between x and y when he has a few observed values subject to error, and has been exasperated to learn that the method gives two lines. Mr. Jessop surveys recent theoretical work that has been done on the problem and shows how a single line may be determined. He also discusses in what practical situations it is appropriate to use the one‐ and two‐line relationships.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Methods for Chemists
abstract: W. J. Youden: Statistical Methods for Chemists. New York: John Wiley & Sons; London: Chapman & Hall, 1951. Pp. x + 126. 24s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Technological Applications of Statistics
abstract: L. H. C. Tippett: Technological Applications of Statistics. London: Williams & Norgate; New York: John Wiley & Sons, 1952. Pp. vii + 189. 18s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Accuracy of Sampling Coal
abstract: In sampling a week's output of 300 wagons of coal in order to estimate the mean ash content, how many visits should be paid to the colliery each week? How many wagons should be sampled on each visit? How many ‘increments’ should be taken from each wagon? What errors are introduced by the techniques of ‘reduction’ and analysis? This article describes some experiments made to provide information on questions such as these, and discusses the application of the results.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Accuracy of Systematic Sampling from Conveyor Belts
abstract: The sampling of coal and similar materials for testing purposes is described, and it is shown how the results of a trial set of observations may be used to calculate the accuracy of a given sampling scheme. The article presents an interesting practical application of the analysis of variance and a new technique based on the theory of time series.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Experience of a User of a Consumer Panel
abstract: Mr. Wadsworth is a firm believer in the value of consumer panels in market research, although he is aware of the difficulties associated with their use. This article is based on experience of the Attwood Consumer Panel, and deals with questions of accuracy and with the practical application of the results.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Introduction of Statistical Methods to Industry
abstract: Dr. Dudding is a pioneer in this country in the application of statistics to the investigation and control of factory processes. Here he describes how he and his colleagues at the GEC developed this application to its culmination in the use of the control chart technique. The article also deals with practical points that arise in introducing this technique into a factory and describes the advantages that accrue from its application.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Measurement of Branch Sales Performance
abstract: This article shows how figures of turnover have been used to measure the progress of a retail shop in a multiple firm relative to that of the firm as a whole, and how the departments in the shop responsible for any trends are discovered. The methods are illustrated by figures for a firm of retail grocers, but they have a wide application.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Scope of Medical Statistics
abstract: In surveying the applications of statistics to medicine, Mr. Bailey deals with subjects of general interest: the use of medical records, the making of efficient arrangements for providing hospital services, and the testing of the efficacy of medical treatments, as well as with the specialist subjects of epidemiology, the efficiency of techniques of medical investigation, and medical genetics. The article is based on a paper given at a conference of the Royal Statistical Society held in Cambridge in September 1951.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Uses and Abuses of Factor Analysis
abstract: Factor analysis, if used cautiously and with a full understanding of its underlying logic, can illuminate many fields of research. It is a statistical method, based on correlation analysis, for combining a number of measured variables into a smaller number of groups of variables: the groups are the factors. They are in no way absolute and the basis of their choice is partly subjective: they constitute a hypothesis that requires to be tested. Examples are given to show the fruitfulness of factor analysis.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Growth Of Reproducible Wealth Of The United States Of America From 1805 To 1950
abstract: The findings of this paper may be summarized in a few paragraphs which, of course, omit all qualifications of the statistical data used. 1. The best single measure of economic growth from the stock (rather than the flow) aspect now available is deflated durable reproducible tangible wealth per head,1excluding military tangible assets, subsoil assets, and civilian semi‐durable and perishable assets. 2. The average rate of growth of real R.T.W. per head for the entire period from 1805 to 1950 is 2 percent, with a range of about 1.8 to 2.2 percent. These figures should be regarded as minima because they do not make allowance for the probable overstatement of the effective rise in the price level involved in the process of deflation and because of the omission or understatement of some types of durable assets such as soil improvement. 3. The rate of growth increased from approximately 2.2 percent in the first half of the nineteenth century to 2.5 percent in the second half. The highest decadal rate for periods of about ten years was apparently reached in the‘eighties with approximately 3.8 percent. From this peak it declined to approximately 1.6 percent for the period 1890 to 1922, but rallied to 2 percent in the‘twenties. From 1930 to 1945 R.T.W. per head not only failed to grow but declined slightly, an unprecendented phenomenon due to the Great Depression and to World War II. 4. Since World War II the rate of growth of real R.T.W. per head has averaged fully 4 percent. This is higher than any decadal rate known; and probably higher too than that prevailing during any previous period of prosperity. The increase of 22 percent in the five years 1946–50 seems to be as high as that in any previous period of equal length. While part of this rapid increase may be regarded as making up for deficiencies in the ratio of R.T.W. to national product created in the preceding fifteen years; and while it is uncertain how long the recent rapid rise will continue, even if we disregard restrictions on civilian capital formation under the impact of rearmament, it may be that the downward trend in the rate of growth of R.T.W. per head in evidence since the late nineteenth century has been arrested. 5. During the one hundred and fifty years for which data are available and which encompass virtually the entire economic history of the United States, the structure of R.T.W. has shown considerable changes, but also a degree of stability which may be regarded as astonishing in view of the extraordinary extension of the economic area of the United States and the radical changes in the nature of its economy. In particular, the proportion of R.T.W. represented by reproducible durable assets for consumers' direct use and for use in production has changed but little. 6. Residential buildings and consumers' durable goods accounted for approximately two‐fifths of total domestic R.T.W. (in current prices) throughout the period, although the ratio has shown a slight tendency to rise since the middle of the nineteenth century. Within consumers' R.T.W. residential buildings have lost slightly in importance at the expense of movable durable goods. The share of government (including non‐profit institutions but excluding military assets) has risen from an insignificant fraction to approximately one‐eighth of total R.T.W.’The proportion of R.T.W. represented by private enterprise (including farms) has declined moderately. Within total business R.T.W. changes, however, have been very substantial. The two outstanding trends are the relative decline of R.T.W. of agriculture (excluding farmers' residences and consumers' durables), and the increase in the share of non‐farm business structures and equipment, particularly prior to 1880. Non‐farm business inventories, on the other hand, seem to have maintained approximately the same proportion to total domestic R.T.W. throughout the period. 7. Until World War I part of domestic R.T.W. must be regarded as being the property of foreign owners. The proportion of foreign investments to R.T.W. of the United States, however, declined rapidly throughout the nineteenth century from a proportion of over one‐eighth at its start to only a few percent after World War I. Investments abroad have never been substantial compared to R.T.W. They have been almost insignificant throughout the nineteenth century. Even at their peak in 1929 they represented only 7 percent of domestic R.T.W., a proportion not yet regained by 1950.

title: Production Functions Derived from Farm Records—A Correction
abstract: Professor W. O. Jones of the Food Research Institute, Stanford University, has kindly pointed out some computational errors which occurred in the article "Production Functions Derived from Farm Records," published in This Journal, August 1944, pp. 566 ff.

title: A Change in the Proportion of Jurors
abstract: In an earlier article in Applied Statistics Mr Corlett described how the proportion of electors liable for jury service can be used as an index of the economic status of an area, and this index has been used in the design of samples. Recently there has been a change in the method of determining liability for jury service, but Mr Corlett shows here that the change makes little difference to the index for the uses originally proposed.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Comparison of Serial Number Digit Sampling with Systematic and Random Sampling
authors: Orville O. Price; Alfred J. McKeon
abstract: This is an interesting example of the application of standard statistical methods to show that the sampling technique described gives effectively random samples in a particular case.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Statistical Approach to a Problem of Chemical Manufacture
abstract: When a chemical reaction takes place in a laboratory the quantities of the chemicals can be controlled accurately to the equivalent weights and the whole of the ‘input’ chemicals can be used up in the products. When the same reaction occurs on an industrial scale it may be impracticable to control the quantities accurately and usually there is an excess of one or other ‘input’ chemical with a consequent wastage and loss in yield. Mr Muir and Mr Downton deal with the statistics of this situation and show how to calculate the most economical conditions.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Technique for Standardising Massed Batteries of Control Charts
abstract: Mr Beer describes a simple apparatus and a method of standardising control charts, with the aid of which a large number of charts can readily be kept under surveillance. He describes an application to production control in a factory.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Advanced Statistical Methods in Biometric Research
abstract: C. Radhakrishna Rao: Advanced Statistical Methods in Biometric Research. New York: John Wiley and Sons; London: Chapman and Hall, 1951. Pp. 390. 60s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Experiment in the Sampling of Agricultural Returns
abstract: This article describes a sampling experiment on the agricultural returns of June 1951 in which national totals for labour, acreage, and livestock items were estimated from one‐half of the parishes. Various methods were used for making the estimates, and the results are compared with actual totals to give guidance for future procedure.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Index of the Volume of Net Exports
abstract: Mr Billewicz argues that for some purposes it is better to measure changes in net exports, that is in exports less the imports required to produce the goods exported, than to measure changes in gross exports. He derives an index of net exports and gives results for the years 1948‐52.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Introduction to Statistical Calculations
abstract: J. Mounsey: An Introduction to Statistical Calculations. London: English Universities Press, 1952. Pp. 351. 15s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Economic Forecasting in Great Britain
abstract: After surveying early attempts to forecast the economic climate with the aid of 'business barometers' and estimates of the national income, Mr Shankleman discusses post war developments in forecasting and gives the results of some forecasts made by a number of economists for the Study Section of the Royal Statistical Society. Some forecasts recently made for 1953 are given.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Estimating Variability from the Differences between Successive Readings
abstract: The method described in this article is not only convenient for use in control chart work but it also allows control chart procedures to be extended easily to situations, such as often arise in practice, in which the ordinary procedures do not apply. Miss Keen and Mr Page give several practical examples in the article and there is an appendix by Dr Hartley dealing with some theoretical points.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Experiences of Correlation Analysis
abstract: Dr Beech uses some experiences in the field of ceramics as pegs on which to hang discussions of practical points that arise in correlation analysis. He deals with the dangers of extrapolation, the use of correlation analysis to test the validity of a theoretical relationship, and the estimation of a constant of proportionality from data.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Planning an Experiment in a Cotton Spinning Mill
abstract: Mr Peake writes: ‘There are not very many published examples of planned experiments conducted in factories, and even fewer published accounts analyse the technical and practical considerations that lie behind the choice of the particular plan adopted.‘ This article is a contribution towards filling the gap.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Quality Control in the Usa
abstract: Quality control is usually regarded in Great Britain as a statistical subject, but Mr van Rest found in the USA a tendency to regard it as a branch of management concerned with the control of quality, control involving not only manufacture to a specification but also a satisfaction of the consumer's needs.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Short‐Term Fluctuations in Petroleum Consumption
abstract: In this article Mr Butler not only shows how he has evolved a method of estimating the trend in petroleum consumption but also expresses the uncertainty in the resulting forecasts by giving limits within which short‐term fluctuations may be expected to lie.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Tables for Biological, Agricultural and Medical Research
abstract: Sir Ronald A. Fisher and Frank Yates: Statistical Tables for Biological, Agricultural and Medical Research. Edinburgh and London: Oliver and Boyd, 1953. Pp. xi + 126. 21s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Theory with Engineering Examples
abstract: A. Hald: Statistical Theory with Engineering Examples. Pp. xii + 783. 72s. Statistical Tables and Formulas. Pp. 97. 20s. New York: John Wiley and Sons; London: Chapman and Hall.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistics and Prediction
abstract: Mr Hughes writes from the point of view of an organisation manufacturing components used on industrial equipment and requiring to predict future demand for the purposes of framing policy. He examines statistical techniques based on regression analysis and the analysis of time‐series and finds them wanting. He concludes that predictions must be based on a considerable amount of reliable, detailed information about a firm's customers and what happens to its products.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistics and Their Application to Commerce
abstract: A. Lester Boddington (10th Edition by A. R. Ilersic): Statistics and their Application to Commerce. London: Pitman and Sons, 1952. Pp. xiv + 451. 25s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Stochastic Processes or the Statistics of Change
abstract: The term 'stochastic processes' is used to denote statistical phenomena that develop in time and the theoretical models that arise in their treatment, and as these are encountered in many fields, this article has a wide practical interest. Professor Bartlett indicates how stochastic processes arise in physics and communication engineering, industry, economics, biology, and medicine. As an example he describes the construction of a mock epidemic series simulating successive measles outbreaks in a boarding school.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Census of Distribution
abstract: The British Census of Distribution has been surrounded by controversy, some of which seems to have been more heated than enlightened; Mr Clark's article sheds light on the subject. After a brief historical introduction there are a description of the Census and some indication of the uses of the results in the broad fields of economics and public policy and in solving problems arising in business.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Design and Analysis of Experiments
abstract: Oscar Kempthorne: The Design and Analysis of Experiments. New York: John Wiley and Sons; London: Chapman and Hall, 1952. Pp. xix + 631. 68s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Economics of Sequential Sampling Procedures for Defectives
abstract: Inspection is undertaken to reduce 'decision costs' incurred through wrongly accepting or rejecting batches of manufactured articles, and for any given type of sampling scheme these costs decrease as the amount of inspection, and hence the inspection cost, increases. Professor Champernowne deals with the problem of determining optimum sequential sampling schemes which minimise the sum of the decision and inspection costs.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Evaluation of Detergent Performance in Domestic Dishwashing
abstract: Statistical analysis is here used in the development of a practical dishwashing test for detergents. Mr Pugh gives a nomogram for assisting in testing the significance of results, and 'power curves' for showing what difference in performance can be significant for a given number of tests, or alternatively how many tests are required to make a given difference significant.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Methods of Statistics
abstract: L. H. C. Tippett: The Methods of Statistics. London: Williams and Norgate; New York: John Wiley and Sons, 4th Edition, 1952. Pp. 395. 38s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Origin and Development of Factor Analysis
abstract: Factor analysis was developed largely by psychologists for use in psychology, but it is a statistical method that has been applied to other fields. Mr Vincent's historical exposition should therefore have wide interest.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Sources and Nature of the Statistics of the United Kingdom
abstract: M. G. Kendall (Editor): The Sources and Nature of the Statistics of the United Kingdom. Volume I. Published for the Royal Statistical Society. London and Edinburgh: Oliver and Boyd, 1952. Pp. viii + 352. 21s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Use of Samples in Auditing
abstract: Mr Williams shows how the ordinary statistical theory of sampling provides the auditor with criteria for deciding from checks on samples of items whether the whole set of items can be accepted as correct. Readers may care to comment on the procedures suggested.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: 50‐100 Binomial Tables
abstract: Harry G. Romig: 50‐100 Binomial Tables. New York: John Wiley and Sons; London: Chapman & Hall, 1953. Pp. xxvii + 172. 32s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Neglected Point in the Construction of Price Index Numbers
abstract: Dr Ruderman calls attention to a point that is of some practical as well as theoretical interest. Moreover, an appreciation of this point will help students towards an understanding of the construction of index numbers.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Accuracy in Statistics
abstract: Statistical estimates are subject to errors which can be reduced at a cost. Mr Hughes thinks that the statistician should do his best to evaluate the errors and state them with the main results, and should determine the degree of accuracy that is economically appropriate to a given enquiry.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Analysis of the Results of a Visual Test of Textile Yarn
authors: Anthony G. Baker; Kenneth W. Hillier; Robert H. Woodward
abstract: By analysing the results of a specially planned set of observations, the authors are able to show that a partially subjective test of yarn quality can give reliable results. The errors are estimated. This article also presents an interesting example of the use of transformations of the observed variable.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Associated Measurements
abstract: M. H. Quenouille: associated Measurements. London: Butterworths Scientific Publications. 1952. Pp. 213. 35s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Exercises in Theoretical Statistics
abstract: M. G. Kendall: Exercises in Theoretical Statistics. London: Griffin, 1954. Pp. vii + 179. 20s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Experiments with a Random Selector as an Aid to the Teaching of Statistics
abstract: Dr Knowles is specially experienced in teaching statistics to engineers and others with an industrial background. The ideas presented in this article are born of this experience.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: International Trade Statistics
abstract: R. G. D. Allen and J. Edward Ely (Editors): International Trade Statistics. New York: John Wiley and Sons; London: Chapman and Hall, 1953. Pp. xii +448. 54s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Investigation of Errors of Observation
abstract: The statistical theory of errors is often applied to measuring the precision of observations of physical quantities without due consideration being given to the question of the independence of errors from various sources, and it is a long time since this problem was examined experimentally. Mr Leslie describes an apparatus with which he intends making an investigation and states some preliminary results.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Le Contrle Statistique des Fabrications
abstract: R. Cavé: Le Contrle Statistique des Fabrications. Paris: Editions Eyrolles. 1953. Pp. 431.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Mathematics in Action
abstract: O. G. Sutton: Mathematics in Action. London: Bell, 1954. Pp. viii + 226. 16s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Methods of Statistical Analysis
abstract: C. H. Goulden: Methods of Statistical Analysis. London: Chapman and Hall; New York: John Wiley and Sons, 2nd Edition, 1952. Pp. vi + 467. 60s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Production Functions for the British Industrial Economy
abstract: In this article Dr Leser examines some data concerning British industrial production, manpower, and capital. His results suggest that whereas for the forty years preceding the first world war the relationship between these quantities can be represented by a simple type of production function, for the inter‐war years this is not so.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Production and Administration Cost in Relation to Size of Firm
abstract: Professor Melman examines some data for US manufacturing industry and finds that at given times production costs per dollar of sales did not vary, on the average, with the size of the firm, and that administration costs per dollar of sales were less for larger firms. The significance of these results is discussed.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Quality Control on the Setting of Voltage Regulators
abstract: Mr Desmond describes the evolution of a quality control scheme which has some interesting and unusual features. It was designed to give the manufacturer greater confidence in the quality of his product, but the practical consequence was that a better product was produced with considerable savings in both manufacturing and inspection costs.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Queueing for Medical Care
abstract: Mr Bailey uses the statistical theory of queues to calculate the number of beds in a hospital, the number and length of clinical sessions, and the appointment system, necessary to provide the corresponding services with a defined amount of waiting on the part of patients. The article is interesting as exemplifying the practical application of modern statistical theory as well as dealing with a subject of general concern.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Sampling Theory of Surveys with Applications
abstract: Pandurang V. Sukhatme: Sampling Theory of Surveys with Applications. Food and Agriculture Organisation of United Nations. Iowa State College Press and Indian Society of Agricultural Statistics. 1954. Pp. xxxi + 491. Rs. 25 or US $5.50.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Social Accounts of Local Authorities
abstract: J. E. G. Utting: Social Accounts of Local Authorities. Cambridge University Press, 1953. Pp. x + 89. 16s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Uses of the Nomogram in Statistics
abstract: Mr Broadbent shows how to construct some nomograms that are useful in statistics and illustrates their use by practical examples.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Design and Analysis of Experiments for Development Research
abstract: Donald Statler Villars: Statistical Design and Analysis of Experiments for Development Research. Dubuque, Iowa: Wm. C. Brown Co., 1951. Pp. xvii + 455. $6.50.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Inference
abstract: H. M. Walker and J. Lev: Statistical Inference. New York: Holt; London: Constable, 1953. Pp. 510 + x. 48s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Methods in Electrical Engineering
abstract: D. A. Bell: Statistical Methods in Electrical Engineering. London: Chapman and Hall, 1953. Pp. viii + 175. 25s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Tables for Aptitude Testers
abstract: A. G. Arbous: Tables for Aptitude Testers. Johannesburg: National Institute for Personnel Research, 1952. Pp. iii + 86. 50s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Construction of Nomograms for Use in Statistics
abstract: Continuing his article started in the last issue of Applied Statistics, Mr Lyle shows how a graphical analysis of experimental results can replace the orthodox algebraic analysis, at least in part.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Construction of Nomograms for Use in Statistics
abstract: Mr Lyle describes graphical methods for constructing ‘empirical’ nomograms. The methods may be used when the equation to be represented is of such form that a ‘true’ nomogram cannot be constructed by the standard exact methods. Part II, which will appear in the next issue of Applied Statistics, will show how the methods may be applied to the construction of a nomogram direct from the experimental results.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Design and Analysis of Experiment
abstract: M. H. Quenouille: The Design and Analysis of Experiment. London: Griffin, 1953. Pp. xiii + 356. 36s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Determination of Single‐Sample Schemes for Percentage Defectives
abstract: With the aid of the table given in this article and of the method described, it is relatively easy to determine single‐sample schemes giving prescribed risks of making wrong decisions. Mr Horsnell also gives an easy method for deriving an approximation to the OC curve of a scheme and for determining confidence intervals for sample estimates.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Measurement of Daily Variations in Absence
abstract: Mr Liddell, who is a member of the Statistical Organisation of the National Coal Board, proposes an index for measuring the tendency of workers to be absent on one day of the week rather than another, and discusses the statistical reliability of his index. He bases his investigations on data obtained from a colliery.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Measurement of Elasticities of Demand
abstract: The measurement of elasticities of demand concerns businessmen and the statisticians who serve them, as well as economists. This is an expository survey of the subject.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Tools of Social Science
abstract: John Madge: The Tools of Social Science. London: Longmans, Green and Co., 1953. Pp. x + 308. 25s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Use of Estimates of Absence‐Proneness for Guiding Executive Action
authors: Adrian Garth Arbous; Herbert Simon Sichel
abstract: This article suggests a way of using past records of absenteeism to select members of a labour force who are suitable for special treatment because they are likely to be absence‐offenders in the future. The problem is a practical one and is so treated, and the argument is not difficult as statistical arguments go. Details of the method are the province of the statistician and are dealt with in an appendix, and in other papers to which reference is given.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Too Good to be True?
abstract: In using the goodness‐of‐fit test should a very small value of χ2 occasion a rejection of the null hypothesis ? Mr Stuart presents the conflicting views on this question and answers it: No.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: What is Econometrics?
abstract: Econometrics, despite its somewhat esoteric title, is about things that are the concern of the business man as well as the economist. Mr Shankleman has written this survey around a review of a current book on the subject.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Who Gets the Medicine?
abstract: Readers will be interested to learn that more medicine is taken by young women than by young men and more by poorer than by richer people. Mr Gray and Miss Cartwright present these and other cognate results of an inquiry, together with the detail proper to a sound statistical report.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Errata
abstract: Our article, "A Fertilizer Production Surface" (This Journal, Aug. 1954), contains certain typographical errors that were not caught in proof reading. The following corrections should be made: In the first equation on page 469, the exponent should be F1. The order of the figures in columns 2 and 4 on page 479 should be reversed, with the smallest magnitude at the heading of these two columns. A negative sign should precede 5.682 in equation III on page 470. In equations 1 and 2 on page 473, the term should read 6.415P−.5. The term in equation (1) on page 481 should read 5.359P−.5. A minus sign should precede 5.7 in table 3.

title: A Design for Testing Several Treatments Under Controlled Environmental Conditions
abstract: Many experimental tests are carried out in controlled environments, but variations within the environment are usually found, even with the most careful control. In this article Mr Clarke shows how a design based on Graeco‐Latin squares was used to balance out the most important trends in a laboratory incubator.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Method of Graphing Sales Figures for Executive Use
abstract: Mr Swan describes methods that have proved useful in an organisation where there is forward buying and, by way of example, discusses the practical implications of the results of applying the method.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Simple Non‐Parametric Graphical Test for the Significance of a Difference
abstract: Mr Billewicz illustrates by some data on birth weights a simple method that should have application in many practical situations. The method avoids a complicated regression analysis and presents the results in a way that can be appreciated by people who are not statisticians.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Application of Linear Programming to the Selection of Raw Materials
abstract: Mr Williams illustrates algebraic and arithmetical procedures by working through two simple examples.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Biomathematics: The Principles of Mathematics for Students of Biological Science
abstract: Cedric A. B. Smith: Biomathematics: The Principles of Mathematics for Students of Biological Science. London: Charles Griffin, 1954. Pp. xv + 712. 80s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Biometrika Tables for Statisticians, Volume I
abstract: E. S. Pearson and H. O. Hartley (editors). Biometrika Tables for Statisticians, Volume I. Published for the Biometrika Trustees by the Cambridge University Press, 1954. Pp. xiv + 238. 25s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Calculation by Accumulation
authors: P. A. Daniell; F. D. K. Liddell
abstract: Mr Daniell and Mr Liddell describe a previously known procedure for calculating various quantities from frequency distributions, and show how time can be saved by using Hollerith equipment.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Current Trends in National Income Statistics
abstract: In this survey of the wide field of national income statistics Mr Saunders describes the various types of data collected and the uses to which they can be put. He also indicates their shortcomings and the opportunities for further work on this subject.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Elements of Statistics
abstract: H. C. Fryer: Elements of Statistics. New York: John Wiley and Sons; London: Chapman and Hall, 1954. Pp. vi + 262. 38s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Fundamental Problems in the Subjective Appraisal of Foodstuffs
abstract: Although subjective methods are widely used in appraising foodstuffs, much work remains to be done in studying the problems involved. In this article Dr Harper gives a general survey of the field and indicates some of the difficulties. He also examines some possible reasons why in some quarters subjective methods are thought to be always inferior to objective measurements, and makes a strong plea for a re‐assessment of the position.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Handbook of Graphic Presentation
abstract: Calvin F. Schmid: Handbook of Graphic Presentation. New York: Ronald Press Company, 1954. Pp. vii + 316. $6.00.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: History of Mathematics
abstract: D. J. Struik: A Concise History of Mathematics. London: Bell, 1954. Pp. xix + 299. 14s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Level of Quality for Minimum Cost of Manufacture to a Specification
abstract: If loaves of bread are required to have at least a certain specified weight, the average weight must be greater than this in order to avoid an undue proportion of loaves that are underweight. The higher the average weight the greater is the cost of manufacture but the smaller is the cost due to the occurrence of underweight loaves. What average weight gives the lowest total cost? Mr Hepburn's article is about this type of problem.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Measuring the Readership of Newspapers and Magazines
abstract: Mr Shankleman has written this article around a review of a readership survey, and has compared the results of the survey with those of another one covering roughly the same period. He has also considered the use of such results in advertisement planning.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Multiple Regression between Variables Measured at the Points of a Plane Sheet
abstract: A correspondent submitted to Applied Statistics a question concerning the analysis of the results of measurements made over a plane sheet of steel. The issues raised were too big to be dealt with in the short space of a Question and Answer, and Dr Jowett has written this article. The article is concerned with the removal of trends in determining regressions and gives a test for patchiness in residuals. It also deals with analysis after trend or patchiness has been removed.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Multivariate Analysis
abstract: In this expository article Dr Wishart brings together a number of related topics that are usually scattered about text‐books and papers. The section headings are: general distribution of estimates of variance and covariance; Bartlett's decomposition theorem; Hotelling's T2; the discriminant function; Mahalanobis's D2; and analysis of dispersion.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Productivity, a Critique of Current Usage
abstract: Lewis A. Maverick: Productivity, a Critique of Current Usage. Published by the author, 701 So. Oakland Avenue, Carbondale, Illinois, USA, 1955. Pp. 30, paper. 50 cents.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Quality Control Through Statistical Methods
abstract: Norbert Lloyd Enrick: Quality Control through Statistical Methods. New York: Rayon Publishing Corporation, 1954. Pp. 72 (paper). $5.00.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Question and Answer
abstract: Readers are invited to submit questions or to deal shortly with points of general interest by supplying both the question and the answer.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Reservoirs of Infection of Pulmonary Tuberculosis in Warwickshire
abstract: In giving results showing the incidence of tuberculosis in Warwickshire Mrs Jessop illustrates what useful information may be derived from a simple analysis of data in the possession of local authorities.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Sales Forecasting: An Evaluation of Some Practical Results
abstract: Mr Liston describes the procedures for making the forecasts required by his firm for budgetary purposes and shows that in the varying conditions that have obtained since the war the forecasts have been reliable enough to be useful. Sales forecasting, he states, is compounded of art, experience, and technical skill.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Makeshift Methods of Analysis Applied to Complex Experimental Results
authors: G. P. Davies; G. W. Sears
abstract: After an experiment on the effect of different lubricating oils on the fuel consumption of motor buses, Mr Davies and Mr Sears were confronted with results that did not conform to any tidy text‐book pattern. This article describes how orthodox methods of analysis were adapted and used to lead to useful conclusions.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Analysis in Chemistry and the Chemical Industry
abstract: Carl A. Bennett and Norman L. Franklin: Statistical Analysis in Chemistry and the Chemical Industry. New York: John Wiley and Sons; London: Chapman and Hall, 1954. Pp. xvi + 724. 58s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Comparison of Means of Industrial Time Series
abstract: When a set of observations form a time series it is wrong to use the ordinary random sampling theory for calculating standard errors. Dr Jowett discusses the problem and shows what should be done.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Design and Analysis of Industrial Experiments
abstract: O. L. Davies (editor): The Design and Analysis of Industrial Experiments. Edinburgh: Oliver and Boyd. 1954. Pp. xiii + 637. 63s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Design of Social Research
abstract: R. L. Ackoff: The Design of Social Research. London: Cambridge University Press, 1953. Pp. xi + 420. 56s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Determination of Branch Staff Establishments in a Multiple Shop Organisation
abstract: Mr Ramm shows how elementary queue theory can be applied to the results of time study in order to determine the number of staff required to give a satisfactory standard of service in a shop.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Productivity Index in Active Service
abstract: Mr Beer describes a productivity index that has been used in a steel works in production planning and control. He deals with the technical and administrative as well as with the statistical aspects of his subject and discusses additional management uses of the index. The subject is treated from the point of view of a works with a large number of varied and changing products.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Intransitivity Of Certain Criteria Used In Welfare Economics
abstract: In this paper it is shown that neither the Scitovsky nor the double index number criterion for an increase in welfare is transitive. The consistent use of either could therefore lead to inconsistent judgements. The relationship of these results to Arrow's general theorem concerning the impossibility of constructing a social welfare function satisfying certain restrictions is discussed. The author feels sure that many of these results must be familiar to individual economists, but they do not appear to be generally known.

title: Errata
authors: M. M. Babbar; Gerhard Tintner; Earl O. Heady
abstract: In the appendix of our Article "Programming with Consideration of Variations in Input Coeffiicients" published in the May 1955 issue of this Journal, there are certain omissions of a formula that make the relative statements erroneous.

title: A 24 Factorial Experiment in Four Blocks of Eight: A Study in Radioactive Decontamination
authors: Martin K. Barnett; Frank C. Mead
abstract: The exploration of complex practical situations is facilitated by the prosecution of carefully designed factorial experiments. Such experiments provide conclusions of maximum generality and reliability at minimum expense. The authors illustrate this by a study of the effect of variations in four operating factors on the efficiency of a decontamination process for removal of radioactivity from liquid wastes, and show how statistically significant interactions were explained by reasonable physico‐chemical mechanisms.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Comment on the Construction of Price Index Numbers
abstract: In an article on index numbers that appeared in this journal Dr Ruderman was led to consider the average price, obtained from family budgets, of a group of commodities. Dr Banerjee shows in the present article that, under certain conditions, an unbiased estimate of this quantity may be computed as the average price weighted with quantities sold in the market.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Regression Control Chart for use in Personnel Selection
abstract: Candidates from different schools may be characterised by two sets of data: ‘scores’ and ‘marks’. Scores, being based on teachers' assessments, reliably measure the relative qualities of the candidates from any one school, but the standard of scoring may vary from school to school, so that scores for candidates from different schools are not directly comparable. Marks are derived from the results of a common examination; they are not as reliable as scores for any one school but are comparable for different schools. Mr Sandon shows how, with the aid of correlation analysis, marks may be used to adjust scores so that scores for all candidates can be made comparable.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Tasting Experiment
abstract: In this article Mr Gridgeman describes the design and analysis of a subjective assessment of the difference in flavour between certain types of jam.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Technique for Studying the Effects of a Television Broadcast
abstract: This article describes a technique, the stable correlate method, for measuring the effects of television broadcasts seen under completely normal conditions. Mr Belson suggests that the method may be readily applicable to various sociological studies of effects, e.g. of television, of cinema‐going, of major events, and of publicity campaigns.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Extension of the Use of the χ2‐Test
authors: Erik K. Leander; David J. Finney
abstract: A recent inquiry about the statistical analysis appropriate to a problem in paper manufacture led to a recognition of a simple extension of the use of χ2 in the analysis of sample statistics with Poisson distributions. Since this extension may not be generally known, the authors have in this article described the test with reference to the actual problem.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Introduction to Stochastic Processes
abstract: M. S. Bartlett: An Introduction to Stochastic Processes. Cambridge University Press, 1955. Pp. xiv + 312. 35s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Are Correlations Any Guide to Predictive Value?
abstract: When faced with the choice between various hypotheses purporting to explain the fluctuations of a certain variable, one is tempted to choose the hypothesis that shows the best correlation with known results in given circumstances and to use it to predict future fluctuations. In this article Dr Ferber examines the adequacy of this procedure in practice.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Chance and Choice by Cardpack and Chessboard
abstract: Lancelot Hogben: Chance and Choice by Cardpack and Chessboard. Volume 2. London: Max Parrish, 1955. Pp. 491. 70s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Contributions of Survey Methods to Economics
abstract: George Katona, Lawrence R. Klein, John B. Lansing and James N. Morgan. Contributions of Survey Methods to Economics. Columbia University Press (London: Geoffrey Cumberlege), 1954. Pp. viii + 269. 40s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Correlated Random Normal Deviates
abstract: E. C. Fieller, T. Lewis, and E. S. Pearson: Correlated Random Normal Deviates (Tracts for Computers No. XXVI, edited by E. S. Pearson, Department of Statistics, University College, London). Cambridge University Press, 1955. Pp. xv + 60. 10s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Examples of Interviewer Variability Taken from Two Sample Surveys
abstract: Most people who carry out sample surveys are aware of the fact that the results are liable to depend to some extent on the interviewers employed. It is not always possible to measure the extent of the variability between interviewers, although steps may be taken to minimise its effects. In this article Mr Gray describes some measurements of variability carried out in connection with two surveys in this country.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Factor Analysis as a Technique for Examining Complex Data on Foodstuffs
abstract: One of the problems arising in the appraisal of foodstuffs concerns the complex relationships that may exist between ‘objective’ tests and subjective judgements. In this article Dr Harper discusses the potentialities and limitations of Factor Analysis in dealing with this problem.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Field Work in Sample Surveys of Household Income and Expenditure
abstract: In this article Mrs Cole discusses ways in which the efficiency of financial surveys might be improved, basing her recommendations largely upon experience gained in a recent survey of household income and expenditure.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Handbook of Industrial Engineering and Management
abstract: W. G. Ireson and E. L. Grant (Editors): Handbook of Industrial Engineering and Management. Englewood Cliffs: Prentice‐Hall (London Agents: Bailey Bros. and Swinfen), 1955. Pp. x + 1203. 128s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Industrial Censuses in Great Britain and the United States
abstract: At the end of October 1954 the Verdon Smith Committee appointed to advise the President of the Board of Trade on future policy on Censuses of Production and Distribution made its report. Some six months earlier an assessment had been made by the Intensive Review Committee of the American statistical programme carried out by the Bureau of the Census. Miss Maurice compares the post‐war policies of the two countries on industrial censuses, which were examined by these committees, and gives tables showing the kinds of information which have been collected.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Missing Values in Experiments Analysed on Automatic Computers
abstract: Mr Healy and Mr Westmacott describe a general technique for dealing with observations missing from block experiments analysed on automatic computers. When more than one observation is missing the technique is simpler than other methods hitherto described and has been proved in practice to be satisfactorily fast. It is applicable to any analysis in which least‐squares estimates are derived.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Modified Control Limits
abstract: The fixing of quality control limits is not invariably straightforward, particularly when the use of modified limits is possible; several methods for calculating modified limits have been described. In this article Mr Hill suggests an approach to the problems that may arise.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Monte Carlo Methods and Industrial Problems
abstract: Although the Monte Carlo method is not new, it has recently achieved a good deal of prominence. In this article Mr Jessop shows, by means of two examples, how the technique may be applied to solve industrial problems, the mathematical solution of which is difficult or impossible.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Population Statistics and Their Compilation (Revised Edition)
abstract: Hugh H. Wolfenden: Population Statistics and their Compilation (Revised Edition). Published for the Society of Actuaries by The University of Chicago Press, 1954. Pp. xxii + 258. 56s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Prediction Methods in Relation to Borstal Training
abstract: Hermann Mannheim and Leslie T. Wilkins: Prediction Methods in relation to Borstal Training. London: HMSO, 1955. Pp. viii + 276. 17s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Question and Answer
abstract: Readers are invited to submit questions, or to deal shortly with points of general interest by supplying both the question and the answer.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Sampling Problems in Connection with Accounting Records
abstract: In this article Mr James describes in considerable detail an investigation into the application of statistical methods to the examination of accounting records. He draws attention to the special problems encountered when sampling highly skew population distributions such as are frequently found in this type of work.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Methods for Research
abstract: Sir Ronald A. Fisher: Statistical Methods for Research Workers (Biological Monographs and Manuals No. V). Edinburgh: Oliver and Boyd, 1954. Pp. xiv + 356. 16s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistics in Hospital Planning and Design
abstract: In this article Mr Bailey shows how statistical methods can help to ensure that hospitals make the best use of limited resources. He deals with the calculation of the size of hospital required, the achievement of efficiency in hospital design, and the use of current statistical returns to check the performance of existing hospitals.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Stochastic Models for Learning
abstract: R. R. Bush and F. Mosteller: Stochastic Models for Learning. New York: John Wiley and Sons; London: Chapman and Hall, 1955. Pp. xvi + 365. 72s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Allocation of Scarce Materials between Products
abstract: Mr Williams outlines the principles of Linear Programming and shows, by a simplified example worked out in detail, how it can be applied to problems of material allocation.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Communication of Ideas
abstract: T. Cauter and J. S. Downham: The Communication of Ideas. Published for the Readers' Digest Association. London: Chatto and Windus, 1954. Pp. xviii + 324. 25s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Dictionary of Statistical Terms
abstract: Maurice G. Kendall and William R. Buckland (prepared by): A Dictionary of Statistical Terms. Published for the International Statistical Institute. Edinburgh and London: Oliver and Boyd, 1957. Pp. xi + 493. 25s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Primer of Social Statistics
abstract: S. M. Dornbusch and C. F. Schmid: A Primer of Social Statistics. New York: McGraw‐Hill, 1955. Pp. vii + 251. 35s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Problem in Production Control
abstract: In this article the authors show how simple statistical methods can help to solve the problem of drawing up a production schedule.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Sample Survey with Both a Postal and an Interview Stage
abstract: In this article Mr Gray shows how a sample survey dealing with the general public was carried out to a large extent by means of a postal inquiry, with satisfactory results. He suggests that the postal approach is well worth consideration for certain types of survey problem.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Simple Sequential Procedure to Test Whether Average Conditions Achieve a Certain Standard
abstract: In this article Mr Tomlinson describes a sequential test which is easy to apply, reasonably efficient, and can be used when the distribution of sample values is not normal.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Optical Periodograph for Use in the Analysis of Continuous Measurements
abstract: Dr Kemp describes an instrument which facilitates the analysis of records of a continuously varying quantity. The frequency of periodic components of variation may be measured and qualitative information may be obtained about components whose period is changing.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Analysis and Presentation of the Results of Factorial Experiments
abstract: In this article Mr Baker describes a systematic method of setting down and analysing the results of factorial experiments. The method is particularly suitable for use with a desk calculating machine and is applicable to either qualitative or quantitative factors.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Calculating the Standard Error of a Proportion
abstract: The author suggests the possibility of simplifying the arithmetical calculations needed to determine the standard error of a proportion and recommends an abridged approach where p does not exceed 01.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Confidence Limits for the Range of a Search Radar
abstract: The determination of confidence limits for the percentage points of a normal distribution is a problem with an exact, but somewhat troublesome, solution. In this article Mr Stevens describes a fairly simple approximate solution, which was found to be sufficiently accurate.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Differences between Experienced Interviewers
abstract: In this article Mr Barr discusses the differences that arose between the results from two experienced interviewers in a recent social enquiry. Although they agreed on matters of fact, marked differences arose over the answers to questions involving an expression of opinion.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Discriminant Functions of Socio‐Economic Class
abstract: The assessment of ‘social class’ is often carried out subjectively, and there is a danger that assessors may differ in their judgements. In this article Miss Gales discusses the use of discriminatory analysis in the assignment of individuals to one of three socio‐economic classes, and describes the results of a preliminary investigation of the problem.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Dynamic Factors in Industrial Productivity
abstract: Seymour Melman: Dynamic Factors in Industrial Productivity. Oxford: Blackwell, 1956. Pp. xiii + 238. 22s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Estadística Descriptiva Y Modelo Matemático
abstract: Enrique Chacón: Curso de Estadística: Volume I, Estadística Descriptiva y Modelo Matemático. Bilbao: Editorial el Mensajero del Corazón de Jesús, 1955. Pp. xvi + 494.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Evolutionary Operation: A Method for Increasing Industrial Productivity
authors: George E. P. Box; George E. P. Box†
abstract: The rate at which industrial processes are improved is limited by the present shortage of technical personnel. Dr Box describes a method of process improvement which supplements the more orthodox studies and is run in the normal course of production by plant personnel themselves. The basic philosophy is introduced that industrial processes should be run so as to generate not only product, but also information on how the product can be improved.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Experimental Design and its Statistical Basis
abstract: D. J. Finney: Experimental Design and its Statistical Basis. Chicago: University of Chicago Press; London: Cambridge University Press, 1955. Pp. xi + 169. 30s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Guide to Elementary Statistical Formulas
abstract: Robert E. Johnson and Doris N. Morris: Guide to Elementary Statistical Formulas. New York and London: McGraw‐Hill Book Co. Inc., 1956. Pp. 102. 22s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Guide to New Zealand Official Statistics (Third Edition)
abstract: E. P. Neale: Guide to New Zealand Official Statistics (Third Edition). Auckland University College Texts No. 2. Christchurch: Whitcombe and Tombs, 1955. Pp. 195. 16s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Linear Programming in Practice
abstract: Much has recently been heard of the technique known as linear programming. In this article the authors show how it was applied to solve an industrial problem; they explain the method of solution in broad outline, without giving all the detailed computations.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: National Income Visualized
abstract: Arthur O. Dahlberg: National Income Visualized. New York: Columbia University Press; London: Oxford University Press, 1956. Pp. x + 117. 28s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Nellie Landblom's Copybook for Beginners in Research Work
abstract: Nellie T. Landblom: Nellie Landblom's Copybook for Beginners in Research Work. Colorado Agricultural and Mechanical College, Fort Collins, 1955. Pp. 118. $2.95.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Normality in Quality Control Charts
abstract: The assumption of normality in the interpretation of quality control charts may be accepted uncritically by some and viewed with scepticism by others. In this article Dr Moore shows how certain departures from normality can affect the probabilities associated with control limits calculated by normal theory. These examples should serve as a warning against the too rigid interpretation of such control limits.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Sampling for Variables with a Very Skew Distribution
abstract: This article propounds a sampling method that may be useful in certain types of survey work, and describes a test of its efficiency when applied to an artificial population with known characteristics.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Statistical Applications of Electronic Computers
abstract: In this article Mr Lipton describes in general terms some of the work that has been carried out by the Statistics Department at Rothamsted Experimental Station, using an electronic computer. He pays particular attention to the routines which have been evolved in connection with the computational work.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

abstract: Sir Ronald A. Fisher: Statistical Methods and Scientific Inference. Edinburgh and London: Oliver and Boyd, 1956. Pp. viii + 175. 16s.

title: The Effect of Obtaining Information from Different Informants on a Family Morbidity Inquiry
abstract: In survey work, information about the various members of a family can be obtained by different methods of interview. Miss Cartwright here describes an investigation designed to study the effect of these different methods on the information obtained in a certain type of inquiry.
