title: The Sampling of Road Traffic
abstract: The study of road traffic may involve sampling problems; in this article Mr Tanner shows how adaptations of fairly simple methods may be used to obtain representative samples.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Scope and Limitations of Econometrics
authors: L. R. Klein; Lawrence R. Klein†
abstract: Dr Klein first considers in general the way in which econometric techniques can be developed to estimate the interrelationships among economic variables, and then deals in some detail with applications of econometrics to particular problems.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Study of Variability in Engineering Design
abstract: Mr Morrison shows how a fairly straightforward application of statistical theory can help the designer. A rough prediction of the variability of a product can be made, and the elements in the design that are likely to give the most trouble can often be identified.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Theory of Games and Linear Programming
abstract: S. Vajda: The Theory of Games and Linear Programming (Methuen's Monographs on Physical Subjects). London: Methuen and Co.; New York: John Wiley and Sons, 1956. Pp. 106. 8s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Three‐Group' Regression Analysis
authors: Wendy M. Gibson; Geoffrey H. Jowett
abstract: Investigators who use regression analysis sometimes complain that the standard least‐squares method is laborious, especially when no calculating machine is available. In this article Miss Gibson and Dr Jowett develop a method originally described by Professor Bartlett, and show that much information can be quite simply obtained.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Use of Regression Analysis to Detect Errors in Measurement of Intermediate Materials in a Multi‐Stage Process
abstract: Mr Stannage shows how certain unexpectedly wide variations in plant efficiency were found to be largely due to errors of measurement.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: ‘Three‐Group’ Regression Analysis
authors: Wendy M. Gibson; Geoffrey H. Jowett
abstract: In an earlier article Miss Gibson and Dr Jowett described some applications of the ‘three‐group’ method to simple regression analysis. The present article shows how the method can be extended to give a multiple regression equation with two independent variables, together with estimates of the residual scatter and the standard errors of the regression coefficients.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Guide to Acceptance Sampling
abstract: This is an expository article, more for people who have to use acceptance sampling than for those who have to develop schemes in detail. Readers who are not familiar with the subject should find Mr Tippett's article a useful introduction to Dr Hamaker's, which appears elsewhere in this issue.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Regression Control Chart for Costs
abstract: Although the idea of a regression control chart for costs is not new, published examples of its use are difficult to find. In this article the authors describe the use of a multiple regression equation to establish a cost control chart designed to help the management in the efficient operation of a freight yard.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Simple Approach to the Minimisation of Store‐Size
abstract: In this article Mr Bailey describes an approach to the problems of checking the performance of existing stores and deciding the size of store needed in new buildings. Since no extensive mathematical knowledge is required in the use of the methods recommended, we hope that some readers may be encouraged to try them out and discover whether they are adequate in practice.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Introduction to Probability Theory and its Applications, Volume I (2Nd Edition)

title: Analysis of Variance of a Randomised Block Design with Missing Observations
authors: William Alexander Glenn; Clyde Young Kramer
abstract: In this article the authors describe a general method for estimating several missing values and analysing the results so obtained. They give explicit formulae for all cases when two or three values are missing, formulae for some other specific cases, and an illustrative example.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Factor Analysis
abstract: In this article Dr Jowett gives a simple description of the essentials of factor analysis, with particular reference to the centroid method. Despite the fact that some statisticians disapprove of the subject, we feel that there is ample justification for an expository article of this kind, so that users of statistics may be encouraged to discover whether this method of analysis is helpful in any of their problems.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Initial Letters of Surnames
abstract: We believe that the information given in this short note is not generally available, and we hope that some of our readers will find it useful.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Marginal Cost
abstract: In this article Mr Muir defines the term ‘marginal cost’, explains a method of estimating these costs, and gives an example of its use. The treatment of the subject is not claimed to be complete, but because of the apparent apathy that exists on marginal costing it is intended to be somewhat provocative.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Numerical Methods
abstract: R. A. Buckingham: Numerical Methods. London, Sir Isaac Pitman & Sons Ltd., 1957. Pp. xii + 597. 70s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Official Statistics of Economic Trends
abstract: In the last few years a number of important changes have been made in the government's programmes for the collection of economic statistics. In this article these changes are summarised in the light of the need to detect changes in the level of economic activity.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Probits: An Application to Observational Data
abstract: In this article Mr Friend first discusses the principle of probit analysis and the strong case that exists for its adoption in industry when a defective rate is being investigated for correlation or trend. He then describes how, in a certain investigation of this type in a steelworks, this intuitive case was borne out by a critical examination of the data and how certain obstacles, implicit in the application to observational data of a technique usually associated with experimental biology, were successfully overcome.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Proceedings of the Third Berkeley Symposium on Mathematical Statistics and Probability

title: Professor Hogben's ‘Crisis‘—A Survey of the Foundations of Statistics
abstract: In this article Mr Lindley combines a review of a recent book by Professor Hogben with an essay on some aspects of the fundamental theory of statistics. He deals in particular with the work of Professor Savage and its implications.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Simple Mortality Rates
abstract: Dr Broadbent here shows how simple forms of expression for the mortality rate may be used in the study of the failure of articles in service. He takes as illustrative examples the behaviour in service of milk bottles and tumblers, and demonstrates the use of techniques of varying degrees of sophistication.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Basic Principles of Sampling Inspection by Attributes

journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Studies in the Variability of Two Blood‐Clotting Tests
authors: Peter Armitage; G. Ilsley C. Ingram
abstract: In a study of the variability of the results of blood‐clotting tests, interest centred on the coefficient of residual variation about regression on time. The coefficient was found to vary from one occasion to another; Dr Armitage and Dr Ingram here show how this variation was analysed with respect to some potentially relevant factors. They also describe the development of rapid significance tests for differences between means.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Economist and His Statistics
abstract: The analysis of published economic data is a function of the growing body of business economists and statisticians. The adequacy of the data for their purposes, some of the pitfalls, and the way ahead for future developments are here discussed in the context of a review of a recent book on British Economic Statistics.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Lognormal Distribution in Quality Control
abstract: In the application of statistical control chart analysis to thermionic valve test data, difficulties have been encountered because of the skewness of the parent distribution. Mr Morrison discusses the generation of these skew distributions in theoretical and practical terms, and describes a modified quality control scheme for these cases; ratio is used instead of range as a measure of variability, and the logarithmic transformation is used to calculate control limits.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Problem of Subjective Classification in Industrial Medicine
abstract: The analysis of the results of subjective assessments is often difficult, and the present example, involving a mass of data, presented some interesting problems. Dr Ashford here describes his approach to the solution of these problems.

journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Significance Chart for Percentages
abstract: Mr Rosenbaum here presents a compound nomographic chart whereby some significance problems connected with percentages can be simply solved.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Statistical Approach to Stores Auditing
abstract: The demand for the exact checking of items in stores can entail the expenditure of a great deal of time. In this article Mr James shows how statistical methods can be used to give results that are sufficiently accurate for most purposes with only a fraction of the amount of exact checking otherwise required.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Adjusting Single Sampling Plans for Finite Lot Size
abstract: Most single sampling plans assume that the lot size is large compared with the sample size, and the calculated operating characteristic curves are strictly valid only under these conditions. In this article Dr Hamaker describes a simple method for finding the sample size and acceptance number appropriate to a lot of finite size, so that the resulting operating characteristic curve closely approximates to that for a given plan with an infinite lot.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Shipping Costs and the Terms of Trade: Australia and New Zealand
abstract: The author shows that it is a very rough approximation to assume that freight rates remain a constant ten per cent addition to export prices. Using Australasian data, he finds variations from six to twenty‐six per cent. For these countries, variation is much greater for export than for import prices, as freight rates tend to move with prices of manufactured goods rather than with basic food and material prices.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Aspects of Quality Control in Cotton Textiles
abstract: It is relatively easy to develop, in theory, methods of statistical quality control; it is not so easy to apply them in the factory. In this article Mr Newbery shows how the problems of developing methods suitable for factory use, and of instructing factory staff in these methods, have been tackled in the cotton industry.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Length of Cigarette Stubs
authors: Percy G. Gray; Elizabeth A. Parr
abstract: In this article the authors describe the conduct and results of a sample inquiry by post. Particular attention is paid to the problem of securing adequate co‐operation and response without unduly biasing the results of the inquiry.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Pattern of Consumer Purchases
abstract: In this article Mr Ehrenberg shows how data on purchases of non‐durable consumer goods can be fitted by the negative binomial distribution, and discusses applications of this finding. He also considers a simple model for purchases made in different periods of time and some quick and easy methods for calculating standard errors.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Use of Edge‐Punched Cards in Statistical Computation
abstract: When punched cards are mentioned, many people think only of elaborate and costly equipment. In this article Mr Ward shows how in certain circumstances simple and cheap equipment may effect worthwhile savings in time and effort.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Use of the Same Experimental Material for More than One Set of Treatments
abstract: Experimental material sometimes has to be used successively for more than one set of treatments. Various ways of doing this are considered in this paper, distinguishing between cases with and without interactions of two sets of treatments.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Weekly, Monthly, and Quarterly Tolerances for Coke Quality
abstract: Mr Ward here discusses an application of the mean semi‐squared difference technique to an industrial problem.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: What is Cybernetics?
abstract: G. T. Guilbaud: What is Cybernetics' London: William Heinemann, 1959. Pp. viii + 126. 10s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Hog Cycle and the Cobweb Theorem
abstract: A surprisingly regular four year cycle in hogs has become apparent in the past ten years. This regularity presents an unusual opportunity to study the mechanism of the cycle because it suggests the cycle may be inherent within the industry rather than the result of lagged responses to outside influences. The cobweb theorem is often mentioned as a theoretical tool for explaining the hog cycle, although a two year cycle is usually predicted. When the nature of the hog industry is examined, certain factors become apparent which enable the cobweb theorem to serve as a theoretical basis for the present four year cycle.

title: A Study of Some Potential Biases in the Operation of a Consumer Panel
abstract: Mr Ehrenberg here describes some controlled experiments with consumer panels in which the effects of length of panel membership and of the number of product‐fields covered were studied.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Application of Linear Programming to Agricultural Economics
abstract: In this article it is first shown how a problem concerning the organisation of a dairy enterprise was set out in linear programming terms. Since the requisite analysis would have been impracticable by the Simplex method without an electronic computer, an alternative method suitable for a desk machine is then described.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Analysis of Straight‐Line Data; Regression Analysis
abstract: Forman S. Acton: Analysis of Straight‐Line Data. New York, John Wiley and Sons; London, Chapman and Hall, 1959. Pp. xiii +267. 72s. E. J. Williams: Regression Analysis. New York, John Wiley and Sons; London, Chapman and Hall, 1959. Pp. ix + 214. 60s.

title: Analysis of Variance of a Balanced Incomplete Block Design with Missing Observations
authors: Hugh Robert Baird; Clyde Young Kramer
abstract: The authors obtain explicit formulae for all cases when two values are missing, formulae for some other specific cases, and a general solution. They give a direct method of analysing the augmented data and an illustrative example.

title: Analysis of Variance of a Latin Square Design with Missing Observations
abstract: The authors here describe a general method for estimating several missing values and analysing the results so obtained. They give explicit formulae for all cases when two values are missing, formulae for some other specific cases, and an illustrative example.

title: Classification Procedures Based on Bayes's Formula
abstract: In this article Dr Birnbaum and Dr Maxwell describe some methods of classification based on the classical probability formula of Bayes, and on some of its applications suggested by the theory of statistical decision functions. The classification problems considered are general, utilising no restrictive assumptions about possible distribution forms, but assuming that distributions can be estimated from large samples of data. The method is illustrated by an exploratory application to preliminary diagnostic classification, using data on 772 patients of a psychiatric hospital.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: International Journal of Abstracts—Statistical Theory and Method
abstract: International Journal of Abstracts—Statistical Theory and Method: W. R. Buckland (General Editor). Published for the International Statistical Institute by Oliver and Boyd Ltd., Edinburgh. Annual subscription £5 (or $16); single copies 30s. (or $4.50).
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Interviewer Variability and a Budget Survey
abstract: This article describes an investigation designed to discover whether the fieldworker introduces a component of variation into the results of a sample survey when the information is obtained from records kept by the informants themselves.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Introduction to Quantitative Genetics
abstract: D. S. Falconer: Introduction to Quantitative Genetics. Edinburgh and London: Oliver and Boyd, 1960. Pp. ix + 365. 35s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Modern Business Statistics
abstract: John E. Freund and Frank J. Williams: Modern Business Statistics. London: Pitman, 1959. Pp. xv + 539. 50s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Note on British Unemployment Statistics
abstract: In this article a Canadian statistician attempts an assessment of the extent to which British unemployment statistics represent the situation truly.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: On Comparing Two Observed Frequency Counts
abstract: A simplified method of calculating the χ2 statistic for deciding whether or not two sample frequency counts came from the same unspecified population is discussed. An alternative statistic is presented; it is based on information‐theory considerations and has the advantage of being simpler to calculate.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Operational Statistics in Instrumental Analysis
abstract: In this article Dr Calder shows how simple statistical methods were used to study some problems arising in spectrochemical analysis. As a result, revised calibration methods were introduced with consequent saving of time and money.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Physical Inventory Using Sampling Methods
abstract: An integral part of any commercial activity is the periodic counting of assets. In some larger establishments this is a major undertaking involving many man‐hours of labour. In this paper Dr Bryson presents a method of continuous inventory in which some quality control methods are used. The basic idea is to use sampling to determine when and where an inventory should be taken rather than use the customary method of taking an annual inventory at a pre‐set time. The results of the application of this method at two large government supply centres are described, and the method is compared with the annual inventory method.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Research: An Annotated Bibliography
abstract: James H. Batchelor: Operations Research: An Annotated Bibliography. Saint Louis University Press, 1959. Pp. x + 866. $10.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Sampling Inspection Tables, Single and Double Sampling (Second Edition, Revised and Enlarged)
abstract: Harold F. Dodge and Harry G. Romig: Sampling Inspection Tables, Single and Double Sampling (Second edition, revised and enlarged). New York, John Wiley and Sons; London, Chapman and Hall, 1959. Pp. xi+224. 64s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Applications of Multivariate Methods in Botany
authors: S. Clifford Pearce; Derek A. Holland
abstract: The techniques of component analysis and factor analysis are here reviewed in relation to biological problems, and their utility in this context is studied by considering their application to some of the problems of fruit tree growth and cropping.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Problems in Applying Statistics to Geology
abstract: In this article Professor Krumbein points out that applications of statistics in some branches of geology are straightforward, but that geologists in general are faced with restrictions on probability sampling, with numerous variables that have to be ‘sorted out’ in terms of their relative importance, and with small‐scale sources of variability that sometimes obscure broader trends in the observational data. Some ways in which geologists approach these problems are discussed to acquaint statisticians with an actively growing field for further application of statistical methods.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Statistical Aspects of Preference and Related Tests
abstract: In the first part of this paper Dr Bliss outlines the nature and purpose of preference tests and, without claiming to give a complete survey, discusses some suitable experimental designs. He then points out the advantages of analysing separately the results from subjects with consistent preferences and those from subjects with no consistent preferences. Finally, he describes statistical methods for the study of sensory thresholds.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Calculations: A Collection of Distribution‐Free and Easy Methods of Estimation and Testing
abstract: M. H. Quenouille: Rapid Statistical Calculations: A Collection of Distribution‐free and Easy Methods of Estimation and Testing. London: Charles Griffin, 1959. Pp. xv+81. 10s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Methods in Biology
abstract: Norman T. J. Bailey: Statistical Methods in Biology. London: English Universities Press, 1959. Pp. ix + 200. 25s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistics and Taste Testing
abstract: In this article Mr Gridgeman surveys some of the statistical problems that arise in the testing of taste. He deals with tests of discriminability and tests of preference, and discusses the question whether the subject should be allowed to record ties or should be compelled to give a definite vote even if he is not sure.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Stock Control Problems: Some Fallacies in Their Current Treatment
abstract: In this article Mr Duckworth has not attempted a comprehensive review of stock control literature. Instead he shows how easy it is for fallacies to arise in the mathematical treatment of stock control problems, with results that might be extremely costly if they were applied to stock management.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Tables of Random Normal Deviates
abstract: J. M. Sengupta and Nikhilesh Bhattacharya: Tables of Random Normal Deviates. London: Asia Publishing House; Calcutta: Statistical Publishing Society, 1960. Pp. xix + 26. 35s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Consistency of Setting of a Machine Tool Handwheel
abstract: An experiment was carried out to determine the effect of certain factors on the consistency of setting of a machine tool handwheel. A half‐replicate of a 27 factorial design was used, confounded into 4 blocks of 16 elements. The design, analysis, and interpretation of the experiment are described in this article.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Economic Incentive Provided by Sampling Inspection
abstract: In this article Mr Hill considers two aspects of sampling inspection—the curative and the incentive. He discusses the importance of the incentive aspect and indicates a possible line of approach to a theory of sampling inspection which takes this aspect into account.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Economics of New Roads with Particular Reference to M1
abstract: This article treats the argument for or against building a new road as a problem in economic prediction, and describes methods of obtaining estimates of the data on which predictions can be based.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Statistical Basis of Quality Control Charts
abstract: S. K. Ekambaram: The Statistical Basis of Quality Control Charts. London: Asia Publishing House, 1960. Pp. x+96. 16s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Philosophy, Method and Status of Agricultural Economics
abstract: The men whom some economists thus tend to despise they call "methodologists." Perhaps they should be despised. What is the use of a man who studies scientific method? He may explain to students how scientists reached their new theories, but this is unlikely to help the students reach new theories of their own. He may generalize, and lay down cannons of inquiry. But there is no technique for forming good hypotheses which can be taught. Discussions of scientific method are often trite, and seldom useful.—I. M. D. Little, A Critique of Welfare Economics, Introduction.

title: A Critical Examination of Simon's Model of Certain Distribution Functions in Linguistics
abstract: In this article Dr Herdan considers some of the pitfalls which lie in the paths of those who construct mathematical models, with particular reference to the study of linguistics. He then shows how some of the properties of word frequency distributions can be explained.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Method of Allowing for ‘Not‐At‐Home’ Bias in Sample Surveys
abstract: In this article Dr Bartholomew describes a method for reducing bias in the estimation of a proportion or a mean arising from failure to contact respondents who are not at home. It depends upon an assumption which has been tested using actual survey data.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Second Course in Statistics
abstract: Robert Loveday: A Second Course in Statistics. Cambridge University Press, 1961. Pp. xi + 155. 10s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Application of Non‐Parametric Statistics to Truncated Selection
abstract: In this article Dr Tallis describes a procedure which saves time and labour when a proportion of a large population has to be selected on the basis of some measurement. Details of the calculations necessary are indicated for the general case, and for the particular cases likely to be of practical interest the results of these calculations are presented in a table to facilitate application.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Introduction to Dynamic Programming
abstract: In the course of their work statisticians are frequently called on to make the acquaintance of, and possibly to use, techniques from other fields, such as operational research. One such technique is Dynamic Programming; in this expository article Dr Simpson explains it in simple terms.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Confounding in 33 Factorial Experiments in Nine Blocks
abstract: In this article Dr Wilkie compares two experiments, one in which interactions only are confounded and one in which two main effects are confounded. Although it is not customary to confound main effects there are instances where it is advantageous to do so.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Dynamic Programming and Decision Theory
abstract: The previous article in this issue dealt with the general principles of Dynamic Programming. In this article Professor Lindley shows how Dynamic Programming links up with certain decision problems in the statistical field.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Empirical Evidence on Optimum Motor Taxes for the United Kingdom
abstract: Using the results of a Road Research Laboratory survey of traffic conditions in Slough High Street the author questions the level of motor taxation. The application of multiple regression techniques to these data, coupled with information derived elsewhere, suggests that the optimum level of taxation may be somewhat higher than the present level.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Interviewer Differences Among Automobile Purchasers
abstract: In this article Professor Jung describes an investigation into the possible differences among the interviewers used in a pricing study. The results also throw some light on the effects of some relaxation of retail price maintenance.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Inter‐Week Variations in Expenditure Recorded During a Two‐Week Survey of Family Expenditure
abstract: The author examines the extent to which, for one commodity (viz. tea), marked differences between the first week and second week records of individual households in a comprehensive family budget survey result in significant inter‐week variation in the average per household of recorded expenditure on the commodity.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Linear Programming in Industry—Theory and Applications: An Introduction
abstract: Sven Darm: Linear Programming in Industry—Theory and Applications: An Introduction. Vienna: Springer‐Verlag, 1960. Pp. viii + 120. $4.75.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Measurements and Standards
abstract: Social statisticians may find it difficult to devise statistics which will prove satisfactory to both administrators and social scientists unless they are willing to recognise and define the place of value judgements in social statistics.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Modern Probability Theory and its Applications
abstract: Emanuel Parzen: Modern Probability Theory and its Applications. New York and London: John Wiley and Sons, 1960. Pp. xv+464. 86s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: On the Price Consciousness of Consumers
authors: Andre Gabor; C. W. J. Granger; Clive W. J. Granger†
abstract: The authors report on an enquiry they carried out in Nottingham, to determine the extent to which housewives are conscious of the price of an article at the time of its purchase. They find that for many of the fifteen grocery products covered, a high proportion of housewives were aware of the prices, although the proportion varied somewhat between the products. The results of the enquiry are analysed in several ways and their implications discussed.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Quality Control (4th Edition)
abstract: Norbert L. Enrick: Quality Control (4th Edition). New York: The Industrial Press; Brighton: Machinery Publishing Co. Ltd., 1960. Pp. 216. $5.50.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Sample Design in Business Research
abstract: W. Edwards Deming: Sample Design in Business Research. London and New York: John Wiley and Sons, 1960. Pp. xx + 517. 96s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Industrial Applications of Multivariate Analysis
abstract: In the past, multivariate techniques have been applied mainly in psychological studies, but it has already been suggested in this journal that this need not preclude other applications. Here we have some industrial examples to support the suggestion.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Notes on the Precision of the Gradient of an Estimated Straight Line
abstract: In this article Dr Beech assembles some material concerning the confidence limits to be attached to the estimated gradients of linear functional relationships and compares them with those for the regression lines. He derives explicit formulae for these confidence limits, and also discusses the accuracy to which the relative error of x and y needs to be known when the gradient is being estimated. The more important formulae are summarised at the end and mathematical details are given in an appendix.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Theory and Methodology in Science and Engineering
abstract: K. A. Brownlee: Statistical Theory and Methodology in Science and Engineering. New York and London: John Wiley and Sons, 1960. Pp. xv + 570. 134s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Stochastic Population Models in Ecology and Epidemiology
abstract: M. S. Bartlett: Stochastic Population Models in Ecology and Epidemiology. London: Methuen, 1960. Pp. x + 90. 12s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Tables of the Hypergeometric Probability Distribution
abstract: Gerald J. Lieberman and Donald B. Owen: Tables of the Hypergeometric Probability Distribution. California: Stanford University Press; London: Oxford University Press, 1961. Pp. vi + 726. ®6.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Analysis of a 32 × 22 Factorial Experiment with Confounding
abstract: This article explains and discusses alternative methods of analysis of the results of an agricultural experiment.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Chosen Few: An Examination of Some Aspects of University Selection in Britain
abstract: W. D. Furneaux: The Chosen Few: An Examination of Some Aspects of University Selection in Britain. Oxford: University Press for the Nuffield Foundation, 1961. Pp. xxvi + 245. 25s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Household Expenditure Enquiry of the Ministry of Labour
abstract: The 1953‐54 enquiry relied on record books for most of the information on expenditure. These records were kept by each household for a period of three weeks, and this paper examines the variation between the three weeks for a wide range of items. The existence of a significant difference between expenditure in the first week and the other two weeks is demonstrated, and the variation in this difference analysed by type of area and season of the year.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Variable Lot‐Size Acceptance Sampling Plan for Continuous Production
authors: D. R. Read; D. W. Beattie
abstract: In this article previously published acceptance sampling plans for continuous production are summarised and certain shortcomings pointed out. A new procedure is put forward in the Variable Lot‐Size Acceptance Sampling Plan, which combines the useful features of lot‐by‐lot plans with the flexibility of continuous plans. The advantages and general features of this plan are described, and a table is given which enables AOQL values to be calculated.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Comparison of the Check‐List and the Open Response Questioning Systems
abstract: The check‐list and the open response systems of gathering information are compared with respect to yesterday's reading of newspapers and magazines and also with respect to yesterday's viewing. There is evidence of sharp differences between the yields from the two systems and also evidence of oddities within the yield from either of them. The findings appear to have important implications for current research practice.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Continuous Acceptance Sampling Procedure Based Upon a Cumulative Sum Chart for the Number of Defectives
abstract: The application of cumulative sum charts to the acceptance of product, as distinct from process control, is described and compared with existing acceptance sampling plans for dealing with continuous production. These new plans are evaluated in terms of their OC curves and, where appropriate, their Average Outgoing Quality Limits. The extension of this procedure to acceptance sampling by variables is also described.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Cross‐Section Study of Marginal Cost
abstract: In this article Dr Oliver illustrates the principles of statistical cost analysis by means of a cross‐section study of the costs of issuing licences by motor taxation offices of county boroughs in England and Wales. The conditions for the application of standard statistical tests are found to be violated, but certain non‐parametric methods are used to confirm that there is no indication of increasing or decreasing returns to scale.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Discriminant Analysis of Reporting Errors in Health Interviews
abstract: In this article the author makes use of data collected for the U.S. National Health Survey in a survey, by interview, of the hospitalisation of persons. Using a least‐squares technique, involving dummy variables, he derives a discriminating function which permits the prediction of accurate reporting of hospitalisation according to criteria such as the income and race of the respondent. The estimated effects of these and other criteria upon the accuracy with which hospitalisations are reported are discussed in some detail.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Method of Analysis of Mixed Level Factorial Experiments
abstract: In this article Dr Wilkie gives a detailed account of the computation involved in a method of analysis of factorial experiments with factors having mixed levels. The methods of calculating the best results and the least‐squares multiple polynomial equation from the significant effects are described, and the type of output supplied by computers programmed to analyse experiments with as many as 52 levels in one factor is indicated.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Experimental Strategy for Investigating Commercial Processes
authors: A. R. Klingel; R. G. McIntyre
abstract: In this article the authors discuss an experimental strategy which has been successfully employed in studying a number of industrial processes. Description of the statistical analysis has been kept to a minimum, as the major interest here lies in the experimental approach rather than in the method of analysis.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Characteristic Functions
abstract: E. Lukacs: Characteristic Functions. London: Charles Griffin, 1960. Pp.216. 38s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Combinatorial Chance
abstract: F. N. David and D. E. Barton: Combinatorial Chance. London: Charles Griffin, 1962. Pp. ix + 356. 63s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Finding an Optimum Target Value in Relation to a Fixed Lower Limit and an Arbitrary Upper Limit
abstract: In this article Mr Bettes shows how simple Normal distribution statistics may be used to solve a problem in manufacturing to a specification and minimising the cost. He gives an example worked out in detail and a table which may be useful to other workers.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Fitting a Negative Binomial Distribution to Coarsely Grouped Data by Maximum Likelihood
abstract: In this article a computer programme used for fitting a negative binomial distribution to a set of grouped frequencies is described. With slight modification this programme can be used to fit any two‐parameter distribution to grouped data by maximum likelihood.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Handbook of Statistical Tables
abstract: D. B. Owen: Handbook of Statistical Tables. London: Pergamon Press; Reading, Massachusetts: Addison‐Wesley, 1962. Pp. xii+580. 70s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Probability with Statistical Applications; Probability; a First Course
abstract: F. Mosteller, R. E. K. Rourke, G. B. Thomas Jr.: Probability with Statistical Applications. Reading, Massachusetts; London: Addison‐Wesley, 1961. Pp. xv+478. 38s. F. Mosteller, R. E. K. Rourke, G. B. Thomas Jr.: Probability; a First Course. Reading, Massachusetts; London: Addison‐Wesley, 1961. Pp. xv+319. 30s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Quality Assessment: A Graduation Method for Improved Accuracy
authors: A. G. Fuller; H. H. Collins
abstract: Two methods are in general use for the evaluation of qualities. The paper suggests an alternative technique which may be employed, and advances evidence to show its superiority over the other methods. The practical application of the technique is illustrated by reference to metallurgical problems and to the assessment of the corrosivity of cutting oil emulsions.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Regression as an Analytical Tool
abstract: In this article Dr Moore first illustrates some pitfalls in the application of simple regression methods to industrial problems, and goes on to discuss the usefulness of multiple regression as an analytical tool. However, when there are potentially many independent variables, the amount of computation for a complete analysis is very large; two short‐cut methods are described here in which much less computation is required.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Statistical Considerations in the Selection of Research Projects in the Pharmaceutical Industry
abstract: This paper discusses the problem of allocation of research resources when a choice has to be made between a number of alternative projects, possibly of differing values. Some general numerical examples are considered to illustrate the consequences in different practical situations of the theory that is developed here.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Some Technical Aspects of a Postal Survey into Professional Earnings
abstract: This article deals with various technical aspects of the survey undertaken in 1958 by the Government Social Survey for the Royal Commission on Doctors' and Dentists' Remuneration. It describes the technique, and discusses the response rates and the question of bias.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Tables for the Distribution and Density Functions of T‐Distribution ('Student'S' Distribution)
abstract: N. V. Smirnov (Editor): Tables for the Distribution and Density Functions of t‐distribution ('Student's' Distribution). Oxford: Pergamon Press, 1961. Pp.129. 80s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Target Distributions for Quality Control
abstract: In this paper the author considers the target distribution concept which is implicit in the high‐precision double‐sided‐limit system of B. S. 2564. The concept is developed and extended to deal with low‐precision data and single‐sided specification limits. Formulae and tables of factors for double‐sided limits are included and a graphical method of dealing with single‐sided specification limits is described.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Testing Two Treatments When There are Three Experimental Units in Each Block
abstract: In this article Dr John discusses the experimental situation where the block size is one greater than the number of treatments to be compared, and gives general directions for the analysis. He describes in greater detail the analysis of a specific problem in which two treatments were to be compared at different levels of another factor, and where the block size was three.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Advanced Theory of Statistics. Volume 2, Inference and Relationship
abstract: Maurice G. Kendall and Alan Stuart: The Advanced Theory of Statistics. Volume 2, Inference and Relationship. London: Charles Griffin, 1961. Pp. ix+676. 132s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Chance Occurrence of Substantial Initial Differences between Groups in Studies Based on Random Allocation
abstract: When the merits of different treatments are being studied and subjects are allocated to the treatment groups at random, undesirably large initial differences between the groups sometimes occur. Incorrect conclusions may be drawn if these differences are ignored. In this paper the authors recommend the use of a statistical technique such as analysis of covariance or standardisation to adjust the results for pretreatment differences.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Sampling and use of Traffic Flow Statistics
abstract: In this article Dr Garwood discusses two main sampling problems arising in road traffic flow statistics. The first is that of the accuracy with which trends from year to year in the total amount of travel on the road can be estimated from continuous counts at a sample of points on the road system. The second is concerned with sampling in time at particular points and the effect of geographical factors on sampling efficiency. One application of this concerns the concept of the economic capacity of the road, the formula for which involves the coefficient of variation of the hourly flows. Values of the latter are given for some typical roads.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Use of Cumulative Sums for Sampling Inspection Schemes
abstract: In this article Dr Kemp describes the use of the cumulative sum chart for controlling the quality of a continuous manufacturing process. Tables are given from which sampling schemes can easily be devised for controlling a normally distributed variable and the percentage of defective items being produced. A comparison is made between some properties of the cumulative sum chart and those of other types of chart.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Correction
authors: Lester B. Lave; Lester B. Lave†
abstract: Robert Solow has called my attention to certain errors in my article, this Journal, November 1962, pp. 941–952. First, where I used the term "manufacturing" in referring to Solow's work, the term "private nonfarm sector" should be used. Secondly, on page 945 the formulae in the third paragraph

title: Potential Uses of Farm Products as Aid to Developing Countries
authors: Willard W. Cochrane; Arthur B. Mackie; Grover C. Chappell; Willard W. Cochrane†
abstract: Excerpt from the Presentation: It is our purpose in this paper first, to refine the concept of the world food deficit by distinguishing between nutritional deficits and economic deficits. Second, we will classify the developing countries into two groups based on current growth rates, and then estimate future food needs for each grouping separately. Third, we will project food availabilities in each group of countries from domestic production and commercial imports and relate those food availabilities to requirements. Finally, we will attempt to develop a strategy for meeting the remaining deficits through food aid. (This abstract was borrowed from another version of this item.)

title: A Guide to Cobol Programming
abstract: Daniel D. McCracken: A Guide to COBOL Programming. John Wiley and Sons, Ltd., New York/London. Pp. 182, 35s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Linkage for Drawing the Normal Distribution
abstract: This note describes a linkage for drawing the normal distribution.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Suggested Method for Constructing Indices of Morbidity
abstract: Measurement of the health of a country or of a group within it has usually been approached by means of mortality statistics, e.g. crude or standardised death rates or infant mortality rate. In this article a method using morbidity data is presented. It is suggested that particular aspects of health and possible aetiological factors may be investigated using appropriately constructed indices; an overall picture is obtainable if a well chosen set of such indices is used. An important consideration is the type of data to be used; reasons are given for preferring that obtained from morbidity surveys.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: An Introduction to Symbolic Programming
abstract: P. Wegner: An Introduction to Symbolic Programming. London: Charles Griffin and Co. Pp. 219. 38s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Ballade of Multiple Regression
abstract: These verses were used by Tom Corlett as a summary of the points which he made in opening the discussion on a paper entitled ‘Multiple Regression and Marketing’ read by John Clemens and Paul Duncan‐Jones at the Market Research Society's Annual Conference at Eastbourne, 13th‐15th February 1964.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Basic Ideas of Scientific Sampling
abstract: A. Stuart: Basic Ideas of Scientific Sampling. London: Charles Griffin and Co. 1962. Pp. 99. 18s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Descriptive Statistics
abstract: Freda Conway: Descriptive Statistics. Leicester University Press, 1963. Pp.189. 12s. 6d.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Effects of Interviewing Designated Respondents in a Household Survey of Home Owners' Expenditures on Alterations and Repairs
abstract: One of the important decisions which must be made in designing a survey to collect consumer expenditures data through household interviews is whether or not to designate one or more specific persons in each household as respondent(s), and, if so, which household member(s) should be so designated. This article presents some results on the effects of designating different household members as respondents, from an experimental study conducted in conjunction with a survey on residential alterations and repairs expenditures by the U.S. Bureau of the Census.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Electronic Computers
abstract: A. I. Kitov and N. A. Krinitski: Electronic Computers. Pergamon Press. Pp. 112. 30s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Identification of Contrasts in Fractional Replicates of 2N Experiments
abstract: In this paper, Mr Nelder discusses the problem of analysing fractional replicates of 2n factorial designs using the plusing‐and‐minusing technique devised by Yates for complete replications. He indicates how to devise suitable systematic orderings on which to apply the Yates technique.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Introduction to Applied Statistics
abstract: John G. Peatman: Introduction to Applied Statistics. London: Harper and Row, 1963. Pp. xv+458. 60s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Introduction to Scientific Inference
abstract: R. Hooke: Introduction to Scientific Inference. San Francisco: Holden‐Day, Inc., 1963. Pp. x + 101.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Maximum Yield for Specified Cost
authors: S. E. Michaels; P. J. Pengilly
abstract: This article discusses the problem of fitting a response surface and finding a maximum of the fitted surface when subject to practical constraints.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Mortality Comparisons in Socio‐Economic Groups
abstract: The author examines various mortality indices as descriptions of the age‐specific mortality of socio‐economic groups and illustrates how undue dependence on an index can be misleading.

journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: On Sequential Sampling from Truncated Normal Populations
abstract: In this article Dr P. S. Swamy points out that, in general, the effect of screening the production, before submitting the lots for acceptance sampling inspection, on the O. C. and the A. S. N. functions of the sequential sampling plan is to increase both the probability of acceptance of a lot and the expected value of the sample number. It is also observed that screening may not be profitable if the unit cost of inspection is high.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Proceedings of a Harvard Symposium on Digital Computers and Their Applications
abstract: Proceedings of a Harvard Symposium on Digital Computers and their Applications. Harvard University Press, 1963. Pp. 332. 120s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Standard Errors for Percentages
abstract: Mr Stuart gives tables from which the standard errors of percentages can be obtained.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Theory of Reliability
abstract: Marvin Zelen (Editor): Statistical Theory of Reliability. Madison: Univ. of Wisconsin Press, 1963. Pp. xvii+166. $5.00.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Statistical Tolerance Limits—What are They?
abstract: In this paper, Mr Burrows discusses methods of setting tolerance limits under different assumptions and provides tables for this purpose.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Tables of Random Permutations
abstract: L. E. Moses, R. V. Oakford: Tables of Random Permutations. London: Allen and Unwin, 1963. Pp. 233. 55s.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Testing for Which?: Some Statistical Problems
abstract: In this paper Mr Mitchell discusses some of the statistical problems met with by the Consumers' Association in testing consumer goods.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: The Significance of Simultaneous Methods of Parameter Estimation in Econometric Models
abstract: The author argues the need for some practical measures as a guide to the estimation of econometric models. Although many theoretical problems still remain unsolved, simultaneous linear equation models play an important part in economic forecasting. This being so, the author's largely intuitive approach attempts to provide a criterion for the selection of the best fit between the ‘known’ variables and the variables to be forecast. Drawing upon an elementary econometric model of the United Kingdom he gives empirical results which tend to support his approach.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Using a Two Factor Analysis of Variance Programme for Experiments with Several Factors
abstract: Methods are given for performing the routine analysis of variance calculations for complete multifactor crossed classifications, nested designs and graeco‐latin squares by using a two factor analysis of variance programme several times.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: Validation Problems in Interpreting Preference Responses to Mixed Food Qualities
abstract: Most foods for which human subjects express preferences are complicated, both in their chemistry and in the sensations which they can stimulate. Responses to such foods are acquired by a long learning process, so that adult subjects have expectations about foods, and about how they themselves evaluate food tastes. In this article Dr Gregson gives experimental evidence showing the effect of expectations on the perception of a relatively simple mixture of tastes; the need to compare this with evidence on perceptual processes in more extensively studied sensory modalities than that of taste is apparent.
journal_name: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society. Series C: Applied Statistics

title: A Model of Economic Development for Peasant Farms in Southern Italy
authors: Gerald W. Dean; Michele De Benedictis
abstract: This study uses dynamic linear programming to derive normative development plans through time for small land reform farms in the newly irrigated Metaponto plain of southern Italy. The analysis indicates great potential for internal savings and investment by peasant families and therefore rapid development possibilities for intensive fruit and vegetable cultivation. However, a risk discount of only 16 percent would eliminate orchards from the plans. Government investment in low interest loans and grants for development costs have a high potential return (about 85 percent) on these farms. To facilitate rapid development rates, technological advice to farmers provided by government appears critical. Methodologically, the model maximizes the present value of future returns from annual plans over a 60-year horizon, allowing capital formation and optimum choice between short- and long-term activities. The model could be easily reformulated to solve problems involving optimum timing of replacement of capital assets.

title: A Proposal for Improving Extension and Collegiate Teaching
abstract: Recently some voices have taken issue to the proposition that the market for agricultural economists may be a declining one. One area sorted out for possible growth has been the extension and collegiate teaching function. However, there are complaints that these activities are not equal in status or monetary rewards to research activities and therefore cannot attract and hold outstanding talent. This article analyzes the nature and cause of this atmosphere of discrimination. It is argued that what is needed is a conscious effort to restore professional status to the teaching function and also to devise ways of measuring and awarding differentials in quality of teaching activities. Among other suggestions, it is proposed that administrators take an active role in developing criteria for measuring the performance of extension and collegiate teaching; that the American Farm Economic Association take specific steps to break down the image of discriminatory treatment between researchers and teachers.

title: A Simplified Approach to Adjustment Analysis Applied to Wheat Producing Areas in the Western States
authors: Fred D. Sobering; Luther G. Tweeten
abstract: This paper develops a measure that serves as an indicator of the pressure that farmers in an area face in adjusting farm size as the price of a commodity changes. The same numerical index can be related to efficiency of land retirement programs, impact on nonfarm firm sales with respect to agricultural product prices, and land price adjustments. Wheat production costs, yields, and prices by regions in the Great Plains and western states are used as a vehicle to illustrate the adjustment index in a simple one crop economy model. No attempt is made in this paper to include all feasible crop and/or livestock alternatives. Conceivably this type of model, with the use of an area composite acre cost, production and price concept, could be adapted for interarea measurement of relevant adjustment pressures in a multi-crop-livestock economy.

title: A Statistical Model of the Demand for Soybeans
abstract: Two-stage least squares estimates of a six-equation model of the annual markets for U.S. soybeans and soybean products suggest that simultaneous measurement of important price-making forces is feasible when the joint product and multimarket aspects of soybeans, meal, and oil are considered. Domestic markets for meal and oil and the export market for beans are considered. Implied direct price flexibilities are −1.30 for farm level soybeans, −1.12 for meal at wholesale, and −.52 for oil at wholesale. Although the predictive value of the estimated model is not yet clear, the empirical results can be used to evaluate the nature and relative magnitude of adjustments in the soybean market as changes occur in market conditions, market structure, and government programs. Both the analytical method and empirical estimates may be useful to price analysts and commodity specialists.

title: Advertising in Social Perspective
abstract: This article examines many of the arguments for and against advertising in terms of the well-being of society. Advertising is a market phenomenon with significant social implications, with a potential for both beneficial and harmful effects. The net effect depends upon the society's ability to institute market rules to reap the benefits while avoiding the harmful effects. Economic theory is of limited value in analyzing the social consequences of advertising.

title: An Application of Equilibrium Size of Plant Analysis to Fluid Milk Processing and Distribution
authors: D. W. Cobia; E. M. Babb
abstract: This article reports the results of an empirical study in which a model for equilibrium size of marketing firm in a spatial market is used. This model has received extensive treatment in this JOURNAL, particularly from a theoretical standpoint. The model is applied to the processing and distribution of packaged fluid milk. Solution of the model results in determination of size of plant associated with minimum average processing and distribution costs. Results of the study indicated that where packaged milk is distributed in semitrailers, in most situations increases in distribution cost as plant size is expanded do not offset decreases in processing cost up to the 134,000 quart per day plant. While total average cost declines within this range of plant sizes, most of the economies of size are realized by the 50,000 quart per day level.

title: An Interregional Activity Analysis Model for the Agricultural Sector
authors: T. Takayama; G. G. Judge; George G. Judge
abstract: This paper attempts to specify an activity analysis equilibrium model for the agricultural sector which would account for the competitive pricing and allocation of primary, intermediate, and final commodities in space. Linear final commodity demand relations are specified and the concept of maximizing net consumer surplus is employed as a basis for deducing the price and allocation equilibrium conditions and formulating the primal-dual programming problem. An algorithm is suggested for obtaining the equilibrium solution and an example is specified which reflects the structure of the programming problem.

title: An Interregional Programming Model for Agricultural Planning in India
authors: Narindar S. Randhawa; Earl O. Heady
abstract: This paper includes development of an application of a linear programming model for agricultural planning in India. The objective is one of allocating production of 16 major crops among regions in a manner to maximize food output from a given collection of resources. Welfare economic restraints are included to assure that cultivators in all regions are left equally well or better off in income level. The programming results provide for important reallocations of production among regions. They result in a projected gain of around 12.5 percent in total food production from given resources, even when limits are placed on the magnitude of shift which can take place. A series of implied prices are derived from the dual solution, to indicate the magnitudes of subsidy needed to attain minimum income restraints in each region.

title: Application of an Optimizing Path Algorithm in the Comparison of Farm Work Methods
authors: W. H. M. Morris; A. Nygaard
abstract: A new approach to the solution of an optimal path problem which is applicable to the analysis of a process or series of processes with several points of entry and end points is presented. The solution of substantial problems can he done by hand. The process must have a definite flow, but by solution at different flows, long-run cost curves for the process may be obtained. The example discussed is the handling of hog manure.

title: Cobweb Models
abstract: In recent years, economists have become much interested in recursive models. This interest stems from a growing need for long-term economic projections and for forecasting the probable effects of economic programs and policies. In a dynamic world, past and present conditions help shape future conditions. Perhaps the simplest recursive model is the two-dimensional "cobweb diagram," discussed by Ezekiel in 1938. The present paper attempts to generalize the simple cobweb model somewhat. It considers some effects of price supports. It discusses multidimensional cobwebs to describe simultaneous adjustments in prices and outputs of a number of commodities. And it allows for time trends in the variables.

title: Cooperative Enterprise as a Structural Dimension of Farm Markets
abstract: This article reports on a theoretical analysis of cooperative marketing under alternative sets of assumptions regarding market structure. For some major results, consider a market where raw material is sold by a perfectly competitive industry to a processing industry. If the processing industry has an atomistic structure and no barriers to entry, cooperative marketing—where some processors are cooperatively organized by raw material producers—may cause departures from perfectly competitive equilibrium in the short run, but not in the long run. Where there is but one processor and blockaded entry, cooperative organization of that firm leads to smaller departures from competitive equilibrium than that associated with pure monopsony under many circumstances. Restricted membership cooperation, however, can give rise to market results that are undesirable from the viewpoint of all except the member producers. If the finished product is sold in perfect competition and scale diseconomies do not exist, cooperative marketing tends to lead to competitive equilibrium regardless of other structural conditions that would support monopsony elements.

title: Curve Fitting to Step Functions
abstract: The methods presented in this paper provide an improved technique for fitting a continuous function to discontinuous step functions so often evolving from empirical studies such as estimation of demand and supply functions by linear programming. Curve fitting to step functions is analyzed using the criterion of minimizaton of the integral of squared distances between the curve being fitted and a step function. The criterion is a limiting form of least squares fit where all points on the step function are treated as "observations." Derivations are given for equations specifying the minimum integral fit for polynomials, a criterion of fit analogous to the coefficient of multiple determination in least squares fitting, and the analogue of the standard error of estimate in least squares.

title: Demand for Fertilizer in the Course of Japanese Agricultural Development
abstract: This study explores the historical dynamism of Japanese agriculture from the side of fertilizer demand. It is intended to extract lessons for today's emerging nations from Japan's experience of agricultural development. The causes of enormous increase in fertilizer input, which played a key role in raising agricultural productivity in Japan, are identified, and their contributions are measured on the basis of aggregative time-series data for the period 1883–1937. Using an approach similar to Griliches', estimates are provided to support the hypothesis of Herdt and Mellor postulated on U.S.India comparative study.

title: Development of a New Approach to Forecasting Demand
abstract: Low income elasticity of demand for food and decreasing variations in consumption due to degrees of urbanization have depreciated the usefulness of income and urbanization for forecasting demand. This article reports some preliminary investigations and reviews earlier research pertinent to the possibility that moderately high income families may act as forerunners of changes in demand and consumption. Also, the author outlines a preliminary conceptual framework for analysis of family consumer behavior. Measurement of the interrelationships indicated by this framework may provide valuable clues for forecasting changes in the way Americans will live as they respond to a wide range of economic, technological, and social changes.

title: Estimates of the Productivity of Management Practices in Local Agribusiness Firms
authors: C. Phillip Baumel; Wayne A. Fuller
abstract: In this study, the level of income and profit of a sample of firms was correlated with indices of management practices and of environment. The indices were included in a production function and a reduced form equation. Management indices were constructed for areas of management defined as personnel and member relations, retail credit, inventory and wholesale merchandising, and strategic planning and sales management. Environmental indices were constructed for personal characteristics of the manager, competition and the board of directors. The indices made a significant contribution to the explanation of both total income and return on fixed investment. The results suggest that it is feasible to construct measures of management behavior and to associate these measures with firm profitability.

title: Estimating Route Assembly Costs
authors: Wallace K. Boutwell; Richard L. Simmons
abstract: Theoretical constructs for estimating unit assembly costs for geographically scattered supplies indicate that for point pickup (involving a trip from a central location to each supply point) the relationship between average round trip distance (ARD) to supply points and radius of assembly area (r) is ARD = 1.7r. However, this relationship is not appropriate for route type assembly. Based on a cobweb type road network where each route covers a wedge-shaped route area, average route mileage increases more slowly than ARD up to a radius of approximately 13 miles and then increases slightly more rapidly. Under certain conditions, route mileage and unit assembly costs can remain constant as radius of assembly area and total volume assembled increase.

title: Evaluating Financial Support of Research Programs
abstract: This study concerns the estimation of discounted, probable cost savings of undeveloped harvesting machinery as a means of determining the maximum financial support warranted for a particular research program. Production and cost standards for future mechanization are synthesized from engineering data. The probability of developing such machinery is estimated through use of the Pascal distribution for a population where variables can take on one of two values: success (1) or failure (0). The particular problem involved is development of mechanical harvesting for olives.

title: Linear Programming Applied to Cost Minimizing Farm Management Strategies
authors: Thomas A. Miller; Charles W. Nauheim
abstract: This paper illustrates use of minimum cost linear programming techniques with reference to a Great Plains wheat farm. It compares the optimum organization of enterprises developed by minimum cost and profit maximizing strategies of management on the same farm and discusses interrelationships between results of these two models. In general, the minimum cost technique may be used to provide the research worker with knowledge of a wider range of possible outcomes than profit maximizing models used alone and hence may be more useful in a predictive sense than profit maximizing techniques. Application of the minimum cost technique to other problems to which it is especially well suited is also discussed.

title: Long-Run Outlook for Agricultural Adjustments Based on National Growth
authors: Luther G. Tweeten; James S. Plaxico
abstract: The hypothesis that emphasis on national growth will lead to continued pressures for farm adjustments in the long run is evaluated in this article. Support for this hypothesis stems from the national goal of adequate food supplies and economic growth. Emphasis on education and research to stimulate growth by releasing technology and skills for high productivity in agriculture can create downward income pressures for farmers over an extended period. Estimates of aggregate product supply and resource demand elasticities provide some measure of the impact of changing technology on income in agriculture and the redistribution of income between farm and nonfarm sectors. While the results of the analysis suggest a likely outcome of rapid growth in farm productivity, this outcome is conditioned by the decision criteria used by society, ability to create new farm technology, future changes in the economic structure of agriculture and weather.

title: Maximum Profit Dairy Rations That Include Wheat
abstract: This article reports some results of a phase of work at Washington State University which was designed to estimate conditions under which wheat would be fed to livestock in the Pacific Northwest, given the prices and nutritive characteristics of commonly available feedstuffs. Solution of the linear programming model developed for this study maximizes returns net of feed costs by selecting both the dairy cow and her ration from the alternatives in the model. Solutions were obtained by parametrically varying wheat price. Wheat was allowed to be no more than half the grain part of the ration. With wheat price at $1.05–$1.28 per bushel, 750 pounds of it would be fed, and with wheat price at $1.29–$1.30 per bushel, 660 pounds would be fed per year. Wheat displaces barley priced at $1.01 per bushel in this solution. If, also, the level of barley use is restricted to half the grain fed, wheat replaces oats priced at $.76 per bushel. With wheat price at $1.49–$1.53 per bushel, 120 pounds of wheat would be fed, and with wheat price at $1.05–$1.48 per bushel, 750 pounds would be fed per year. In this solution, corn, milo, and wheat millfeed also were very sensitive to price competition with oats and with wheat priced between $1.50 and $1.55 per bushel.

title: Milk Production Functions Incorporating Variables for Cow Characteristics and Environment
authors: Earl O. Heady; N. L. Jacobson; A. E. Freeman; J. Patrick Madden
abstract: This study provides estimates of the milk production function as it relates to feed inputs, cow characteristics and environmental variables. It is based on two physical experiments with 72 Holstein cows. The regression equation used included estimation of 27 coefficients for the variables: weekly hay consumption, weekly grain consumption, stage of lactation, cow ability, index of inbreeding, body weight, age of cow, index of cow maturity and environmental temperature. Various physical and economic relationships estimated for the function include isoquants, isoclines, marginal quantities and profit maximizing levels of various variables. Confidence limits estimated for isoquants, isoclines and optimizing feed inputs suggest greater certainty in grain recommendations than for hay recommendations. The study, while not considered final in respect to coefficients for numerous variables, suggests that with further research, many aspects of dairy cattle breeding, nutrition and management can be incorporated into a single estimated production function.

title: Monthly Price-Quantity Relations for Broilers at the Farm Level
